**Royal Air Maroc** (**RAM**, French: [[ʁwajal ɛːʁ maʁɔk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الخطوط الملكية المغربية, [romanised](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *al-Khuṭūṭu l-Malakiyyatu l-Maghribiyyah*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'Royal Moroccan [Air]Lines') is the [Moroccan](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") [national carrier](/wiki/National_carrier "National carrier"),[[5]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_to_launch_787_services_on_Feb._22-5) as well as the country's largest airline,[[6]](#cite_note-Ryanair_opens_two_new_bases_in_Morocco,_6_months_after_charges_dispute;_and_RAM_looks_for_a_partner-6) ranking among the largest in Africa.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

RAM is wholly owned by the [Moroccan Government](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Morocco "Cabinet of Morocco"), and has its headquarters on the grounds of [Casablanca-Anfa Airport](/wiki/Casablanca-Anfa_Airport "Casablanca-Anfa Airport"). It joined the [Oneworld](/wiki/Oneworld "Oneworld") alliance in 2020.

From its base at [Mohammed V International Airport](/wiki/Mohammed_V_International_Airport "Mohammed V International Airport"),[[8]](#cite_note-airline-news-19-8) the carrier operates a domestic network in Morocco, scheduled international flights to [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), and [North](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), and occasional [charter flights](/wiki/Charter_flight "Charter flight") that include [Hajj](/wiki/Hajj "Hajj") services.[[9]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc,_pilot_union_reach_agreement_to_end_dispute-9) As of November 2023, the airline serves 45 countries and 134 routes.[[3]](#cite_note-cha-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Royal_Air_Maroc_Caravelle_Haafke.jpg/250px-Royal_Air_Maroc_Caravelle_Haafke.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Air_Maroc_Caravelle_Haafke.jpg)

Royal Air Maroc Caravelle at [Düsseldorf Airport](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf_Airport "Düsseldorf Airport") in 1973. The carrier ordered its first two aircraft of the type in 1958.[[10]](#cite_note-Flight1958-101-10): 101

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_727-200Adv_CN-CCH_DUS_1993-4-14.png/250px-Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_727-200Adv_CN-CCH_DUS_1993-4-14.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_727-200Adv_CN-CCH_DUS_1993-4-14.png)

Royal Air Maroc [Boeing 727-200 Advanced](/wiki/Boeing_727-200_Advanced "Boeing 727-200 Advanced") at [Düsseldorf Airport](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf_Airport "Düsseldorf Airport") in 1993.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_747-200BM_CN-RME_CDG_Jul_1996.png/250px-Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_747-200BM_CN-RME_CDG_Jul_1996.png)](/wiki/File:Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_747-200BM_CN-RME_CDG_Jul_1996.png)

Royal Air Maroc [Boeing 747-200B](/wiki/Boeing_747-200B "Boeing 747-200B") at [Charles de Gaulle Airport](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport") in 1996.

*Royal Air Maroc—Compagnie Nationale de Transports Aériens* was formed in July 1953 as a result of the merger of *Compagnie Chérifienne de l'Air ([Air Atlas](/wiki/Air_Atlas "Air Atlas"))* — set up in 1946 with [Junkers Ju 52s](/wiki/Junkers_Ju_52 "Junkers Ju 52") — and *Compagnie Chérifienne de Transports Aériens Air Maroc*, that was founded in 1947 and commenced scheduled operations in 1949.[[11]](#cite_note-Flight1960-509-11)

The fleet of the newly formed airline included six [Bretagnes](/wiki/Sud-Ouest_Bretagne "Sud-Ouest Bretagne"), four [Commandos](/wiki/Curtiss_C-46_Commando "Curtiss C-46 Commando"), five [DC-3s](/wiki/Douglas_DC-3 "Douglas DC-3") and two [Languedocs](/wiki/SNCASE_Languedoc "SNCASE Languedoc").[[12]](#cite_note-Flight1953-468-12) These aircraft worked on routes previously served by the predecessor companies, and added the cities of [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva") and [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13)

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

The name *Royal Air Maroc* was adopted on 28 June 1957,[[nb 1]](#cite_note-14) with the [government of Morocco](/wiki/Government_of_Morocco "Government of Morocco") having a 67.73% stake.[[14]](#cite_note-RAM_prépare_ces_50_ans_d'existence-15) [Hajj](/wiki/Hajj "Hajj") flights commenced in 1957.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13)

The [carrier](/wiki/Air_carrier "Air carrier")'s fleet comprised 16 aircraft by April 1958, including four [DC-4s](/wiki/Douglas_DC-4 "Douglas DC-4"), three DC-3s, seven Bretagnes and two [C-46s](/wiki/Curtiss_C-46_Commando "Curtiss C-46 Commando").[[15]](#cite_note-Flight1958-546-16) In May 1958, the airline ordered two [Caravelles](/wiki/Sud_Aviation_Caravelle "Sud Aviation Caravelle").[[10]](#cite_note-Flight1958-101-10): 101 In July, a number of long-haul routes were launched using four [Lockheed L-749 Constellations](/wiki/Lockheed_L-749_Constellation "Lockheed L-749 Constellation") [leased](/wiki/Aircraft_lease "Aircraft lease") from [Air France](/wiki/Air_France "Air France"), and the coastal [Oran](/wiki/Oran "Oran")–[Oujda](/wiki/Oujda "Oujda") run — which had been suspended in May — was reopened. Also in 1958, the carrier started flying to [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar"). The arrival of the Constellations enabled the airline to withdraw the DC-4s from service.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13)

A single Caravelle was part of the fleet of four [L-749 Constellations](/wiki/Lockheed_L-749_Constellation "Lockheed L-749 Constellation"), four DC-4s and three DC-3s by April 1960, making the Caravelle the first jet aircraft operated by the company; another Caravelle was yet to be delivered.[[11]](#cite_note-Flight1960-509-11) The type began serving the [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat "Rabat")–[Bamako](/wiki/Bamako "Bamako") route in July 1961. By 1964, there were three Caravelles in the fleet.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13) A fourth was ordered in late 1964.[[16]](#cite_note-FI1964-990-17)

In April 1965, the company had 758 employees and chairmanship was held by Mohammed Al Fassi. The route network included services within [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), and also linked North Africa with [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"); the [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca")–[Dakar](/wiki/Dakar "Dakar") and Casablanca–[Las Palmas](/wiki/Las_Palmas "Las Palmas") sectors were also flown. Shareholding at the time was split between the government of Morocco (64%), Air France (21%), [Compagnie Generale Transatlantique](/wiki/Compagnie_Generale_Transatlantique "Compagnie Generale Transatlantique") (7.6%), [Aviacion y Comercio](/wiki/Aviaco "Aviaco") (5%) and others (2.4%).[[17]](#cite_note-FI1965-598-18) An order for a fifth Caravelle was placed in early 1968.[[18]](#cite_note-FI1968-44-19) By 1969, all routes to Europe and North Africa were flown using solely these aircraft.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s")]

In 1969, the carrier placed its first order with [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing").[[20]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_to_Acquire_Next-generation_737s-21) Royal Air Maroc took delivery of the first Boeing aircraft, a [Boeing 727-200](/wiki/Boeing_727-200 "Boeing 727-200"), in 1970,[[21]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_Receives_Its_First_Extended-Range_Boeing_767-300-22) with the carrier deploying it on revenue service on 15 May.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13)

Subsidiary airline *Royal Air Inter* was formed early in 1970 to undertake domestic routes using [Fokker F-27 Friendship](/wiki/Fokker_F-27_Friendship "Fokker F-27 Friendship") equipment; this sister company started operations on 2 April 1970, and by May 1971, it was serving [Agadir](/wiki/Agadir "Agadir"), [Al Hoceima](/wiki/Al_Hoceima "Al Hoceima"), Casablanca, [Fez](/wiki/Fes "Fes"), [Marrakesh](/wiki/Marrakesh "Marrakesh"), Oujda, Rabat, [Tangier](/wiki/Tangier "Tangier") and [Tetouan](/wiki/Tetouan "Tetouan").[[22]](#cite_note-FI1971-641-23) The RAM's fleet at May 1971 comprised two Boeing 727-200s, along with four [Caravelles](/wiki/Sud_Aviation_Caravelle "Sud Aviation Caravelle") and two [SIAI Marchetti SF.260s](/wiki/SIAI_Marchetti_F.260 "SIAI Marchetti F.260").[[23]](#cite_note-FI1971-642-24) At a cost of [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")8.85 million, a third Boeing 727-200 was ordered in 1972.[[24]](#cite_note-FI1982-487-25) In 1974, the carrier ordered a single [Boeing 727-200 Advanced](/wiki/Boeing_727-200_Advanced "Boeing 727-200 Advanced"),[[25]](#cite_note-FI1974-589-26) followed by an order for a fourth Boeing 727-200.[[26]](#cite_note-FI1974-516-27) Also that year, negotiations with Air France for the lease of a [Boeing 707-320B](/wiki/Boeing_707-320B "Boeing 707-320B") started.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13) By March 1975, the Boeing 707 was part of an 11-strong fleet, along with four Boeing 727-200s, four Caravelles, and two SIAI Marchetti SF.260s.[[27]](#cite_note-FI1975-499-28) RAM flew the leased Boeing 707 to [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") for the first time in April 1975, becoming the first Arab airline in serving this destination.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998128-13) During the year, the company acquired three [Boeing 737-200s](/wiki/Boeing_737-200 "Boeing 737-200") to replace the Caravelles.[[28]](#cite_note-FI1975-692-29) Also in 1975, a weekly non-stop service to [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") was started.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998129-30) An order for three more Boeing 727-200s was placed in early 1976.[[30]](#cite_note-FI1976-629-31) That year, the four Caravelles were withdrawn from service and sold. A [Boeing 747-200B](/wiki/Boeing_747-200B "Boeing 747-200B") entered the fleet in September 1978.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuttery1998129-30)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1980s")]

By July 1980, Royal Air Maroc had 3,583 employees. At this time, the carrier's fleet consisted of a single [Boeing 747-200B](/wiki/Boeing_747-200B "Boeing 747-200B"), two Boeing 707-320Cs, one [Boeing 707-320](/wiki/Boeing_707-320 "Boeing 707-320"), seven Boeing 727-200s and three Boeing 737-200s.[[31]](#cite_note-FI1980-349-32) Another Boeing 727-200, ordered in January that year,[[32]](#cite_note-FI1980-6-33) was still pending delivery.[[31]](#cite_note-FI1980-349-32) At a cost of US$16 million, an additional Boeing 737-200 was ordered in 1981, with the [US Export-Import Bank](/wiki/US_Export-Import_Bank "US Export-Import Bank") arranging a US$5 million loan to secure the delivery, and RAM and private financers funding the balance. Delivery was slated for March 1982.[[33]](#cite_note-FI1981-1294-34) During 1982, two [Boeing 737-200Cs](/wiki/Boeing_737-200C "Boeing 737-200C") were ordered for US$33 million; deliveries were arranged for March and June 1983.[[34]](#cite_note-FI1982-186-35) Late that year, the airline joined the [International Air Transport Association](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association "International Air Transport Association").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

In July 1986, RAM was the first [African](/wiki/Africa "Africa") airline to put the [Boeing 757](/wiki/Boeing_757 "Boeing 757") in service.[[36]](#cite_note-Background-37) The first of these aircraft that was delivered to the company set a record for the type when it flew the distance separating [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle") from Casablanca, 4,910 nautical miles (9,090 km; 5,650 mi), non-stop.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1990s")]

In the early days of the decade, the last of the Boeing 707s was removed from the fleet. Meanwhile, newer, more efficient, Classic 400 and 500 series Boeing 737s were introduced to increase the frequency of European routes. By the middle of the decade all 727s had disappeared. To consolidate its North American operations, Royal Air Maroc purchased a single [Boeing 747-400](/wiki/Boeing_747-400 "Boeing 747-400"). As the decade progressed, new routes to previously under-served African airports were opened.

### 2000–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2000–present")]

With the increasing number of passengers and newly opened routes as well as increasing oil prices, there was a need to buy new aircraft. In 2000, an order for 20 Boeing 737 Next Generation aircraft and 4 [Airbus A321s](/wiki/Airbus_A321 "Airbus A321") was placed. Meanwhile, more routes to the west and central African cities were opened.
RAM was now changing, from providing flights to meet the demands of foreign tourists and Moroccan expatriates, to providing connections between European cities and African cities via the Casablanca hub. In 2002, the company leased two 767s to replace the single 747 in North American routes.

Morocco and the EU signed an [open skies](/wiki/Open_skies "Open skies") agreement in late 2006. This means that Royal Air Maroc will have to face tough competition from low-cost carriers eager to exploit profitable routes between [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") and Morocco. A further challenge arises from the high cost of kerosene and the fact that the company may have to drop some of its unprofitable domestic and international routes.

Royal Air Maroc became [Oneworld](/wiki/Oneworld "Oneworld")'s 14th member on 1 April 2020.[[4]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_to_join_oneworld-4)[[38]](#cite_note-AW-39)[[1]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_sees_fleet,_hub_growth_ahead_of_oneworld_membership-1)[[39]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_to_become_14th_oneworld_member-40)

In June 2023, the airline announced a substantial expansion plan with the goal of doubling the size of its fleet over the next decade and adding international connections with new routes to Europe. This plan was revealed by the RAM's [chief executive officer](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") (CEO), Abdelhamid Addou.[[40]](#cite_note-41) In October, the airline announced plans to purchase 200 planes within a decade through a tender, aiming to meet the demand driven by the [2030 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2030_FIFA_World_Cup "2030 FIFA World Cup") and strengthen its presence in the growing African market.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-7)

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit), the airline is owned by the Moroccan government; 53.94% of shares are owned directly by the state, an additional 44.10% are held via the Hassan II Fund for Economic and Social Development.[[42]](#cite_note-Qatar_Airways_considers_investment_in_Royal_Air_Maroc-43) The remaining 2% are owned by private investors including [Air France](/wiki/Air_France "Air France") and [Iberia](/wiki/Iberia_(airline) "Iberia (airline)").[[43]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_-_Gouvernance-44)

The government has considered the [privatisation](/wiki/Privatisation "Privatisation") of the company for about 20 years;[[44]](#cite_note-Morocco_Seeking_Airline_Partner_For_RAM-45) the latest plan, dating from late 2012, reportedly included selling up to 44% of the stakes to a [Gulf](/wiki/Arab_States_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab States of the Persian Gulf") airline.[[6]](#cite_note-Ryanair_opens_two_new_bases_in_Morocco,_6_months_after_charges_dispute;_and_RAM_looks_for_a_partner-6)

* [RAM Cargo](/wiki/RAM_Cargo "RAM Cargo")

* [RAM Academy](/w/index.php?title=RAM_Academy&action=edit&redlink=1 "RAM Academy (page does not exist)")

Former RAM subsidiaries include:

* *Air Gabon International*, formed in December 2005 as a [joint venture](/wiki/Joint_venture "Joint venture") between the [State of Gabon](/wiki/Gabon "Gabon") and RAM, which held a controlling interest (51%).[[46]](#cite_note-Création_de_Air_Gabon_International_par_RAM_et_Air_Gabon-47)[[47]](#cite_note-RAM_Sets_Up_Gabon_Airline_JV-48) It intended to be the new [Gabonese](/wiki/Gabon "Gabon") flag carrier.[[48]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_helps_launch_Gabon-based_airline-49)
* *[Air Sénégal International](/wiki/Air_S%C3%A9n%C3%A9gal_International "Air Sénégal International")*, created in 2000, had its maiden flight in 2001;[[49]](#cite_note-Air_Senegal_shuts_down_operations_in_RAM_row-50) the [government of Senegal](/wiki/Government_of_Senegal "Government of Senegal") was the stockholder of 49% of the company and RAM held the balance at the time it ceased operations in April 2009.[[50]](#cite_note-Other_News_-_04/27/2009-51)[[51]](#cite_note-Air_Senegal's_passengers_stranded-52)
* *Amadeus Morocco*[[52]](#cite_note-Ambitions_in_Africa-53)
* *[Atlas Blue](/wiki/Atlas_Blue "Atlas Blue")*: RAM's fully owned low-cost subsidiary.[[53]](#cite_note-Other_News_-_06/13/2006-54) It was created on 28 May 2004,[[54]](#cite_note-Un_Boeing_737-800_pour_renforcer_la_flotte_d'_'Atlas_Blue'-55) and started operations in July the same year.[[55]](#cite_note-RAM_repositioning_to_contend_with_European_competition-56) Based in [Marrakesh](/wiki/Marrakesh "Marrakesh"), it initially operated a single [Boeing 737-400](/wiki/Boeing_737-400 "Boeing 737-400") that was transferred from its parent company and deployed on charter routes to [France](/wiki/France "France").[[56]](#cite_note-Moroccan_low-cost_carrier_starts_flying-57) Operations were integrated into RAM in 2009,[[57]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_signs_MOU_to_take_majority_stake_in_TUI's_Jet4You-58) while the fleets of both carriers officially merged on 10 February 2011.[[41]](#cite_note-La_direction_d'Atlas_Blue_déplore_la_gréve_et_l'occupation_de_ses_locaux_par_le_personnel-42)
* *Atlas Catering Airlines Services*
* *[Atlas Hospitality Morocco](/wiki/Atlas_Hospitality "Atlas Hospitality")*,[[58]](#cite_note-Restauration_:_7_opérateurs_internationaux_courtisent_Atlas_Catering_!-59) a chain of hotels[[59]](#cite_note-60)
* *Matis*, dedicated to the aircraft wiring industry[[58]](#cite_note-Restauration_:_7_opérateurs_internationaux_courtisent_Atlas_Catering_!-59)

### Business trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Business trends")]

The carrier achieved the best result in ten years[[60]](#cite_note-RAM,_takes_off-61) for the [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") 2012; cost-cutting measures had included the reduction in the number of employees by 1,974 between June 2011 and October 2012 and a fleet renewal program, and the [net loss](/wiki/Net_income "Net income") for the same period was reduced to [MAD](/wiki/Moroccan_dirham "Moroccan dirham")43 million.[[61]](#cite_note-BMCE-62) Following restructuring, which included the removal of ten [medium-haul](/wiki/Medium-haul "Medium-haul") aircraft, the staff-to-aircraft ratio decreased from 110:1 to 58:1, whereas the ratio of passengers transported per employee increased from 1,054:1 to 2,329:1.[[62]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_receives_first_787_as_the_airline_finishes_restructuring_&_returns_to_profitability-63) In 2019, Royal Air Maroc made a net profit of US$51.9 million.[[63]](#cite_note-:0-64)

### Head office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Head office")]

Royal Air Maroc has its head office on the grounds of Casablanca-Anfa Airport in Casablanca.[[85]](#cite_note-88) In 2004 the airline announced that it would move its head office from Casablanca to the [Nouaceur Province](/wiki/Nouaceur_Province "Nouaceur Province"), near Mohammed V International Airport. MAP, the official state news agency, said that the construction of the headquarters and a 500-room conference hotel would take 1 year and 6 months.[[86]](#cite_note-HQmove-89) The agreement to build the head office in Nouaceur was signed in 2009.[[87]](#cite_note-90)

### Recent developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Recent developments")]

As of March 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit), Royal Air Maroc (RAM) operated an all-[Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing") fleet.[[120]](#cite_note-ATDB-123) RAM placed an order for nine [Boeing 737 Next Generation](/wiki/Boeing_737_Next_Generation "Boeing 737 Next Generation") in October 1996; the first of these aircraft the airline took possession of, in July 1998, was a [Boeing 737-800](/wiki/Boeing_737-800 "Boeing 737-800"), making the carrier the first scheduled one outside the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to take delivery of this model.[[121]](#cite_note-Royal_air_Maroc_Takes_the_Lead_with_its_First_Boeing_737-800-124) RAM received its first [Boeing 737-700](/wiki/Boeing_737-700 "Boeing 737-700") in April 1999.[[122]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_Accepts_Its_First_Boeing_Next-Generation_737-700-125) In March 2001, RAM placed orders for 20 new [Boeing 737NGs](/wiki/Boeing_737NG "Boeing 737NG") plus two [wide-bodied](/wiki/Wide-body_aircraft "Wide-body aircraft") [Boeing 767-300ERs](/wiki/Boeing_767-300ER "Boeing 767-300ER") in a deal worth about US$1.4 billion.[[123]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_Orders_More_Boeing_Next-Generation_737s-126)[[124]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_to_Expand_its_Fleet_with_New_Boeing_Airplanes-127) That same year, RAM became a new [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus") customer when it bought four [Airbus A321s](/wiki/Airbus_A321 "Airbus A321").[[125]](#cite_note-Airbus_and_Boeing_Stage_a_Dogfight_at_Paris_Air_Show-128) In January 2002, the airline took delivery of its first Boeing 767-300ER.[[21]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_Receives_Its_First_Extended-Range_Boeing_767-300-22)

After the carrier's Board of Directors agreed to buy a number of [Boeing 787s](/wiki/Boeing_787_Dreamliner "Boeing 787 Dreamliner") on 29 July 2005,[[126]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_Selects_Boeing_787_Dreamliner-129) a [memorandum of understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding "Memorandum of understanding") for the acquisition of these aircraft was signed with [Boeing](/wiki/Boeing "Boeing") on 31 Jul the same year.[[127]](#cite_note-RAM_signs_MOU_for_up_to_five_Dreamliners-130) The deal, worth [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")650 million and including five [Dreamliners](/wiki/Boeing_787_Dreamliner "Boeing 787 Dreamliner"), was confirmed in early November that year, with initial delivery slated for October 2008.[[128]](#cite_note-Other_News_-_11/04/2005-131) The purchase contract was signed in December 2005, and also included an aircraft of the type on option.[[129]](#cite_note-Other_News_-_12/07/2005-132) Following an over-US$100 million-worth contract that was signed in February 2006, these aircraft will be powered with [General Electric GEnx](/wiki/General_Electric_GEnx "General Electric GEnx") engines.[[130]](#cite_note-Other_News_-_02/24/2006-133) Boeing delivered RAM's first Dreamliner in December 2014.[[131]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_receives_its_first_Boeing_787-134)[[132]](#cite_note-Boeing,_Royal_Air_Maroc_Celebrate_Arrival_of_Airline's_First_787_Dreamliner-135)

RAM was the launch customer for the [ATR 72-600](/wiki/ATR_72-600 "ATR 72-600"), when it took delivery of two of these aircraft, on behalf of its regional subsidiary [RAM Express](/wiki/RAM_Express "RAM Express"), in August 2011.[[133]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_takes_delivery_of_first_two_ATR_72s-136)[[134]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_takes_delivery_of_first_ATR_72-600-137) The carrier had placed an order for four aircraft of the type in March 2009, along with two [ATR 42-600s](/wiki/ATR_42-600 "ATR 42-600").[[135]](#cite_note-Other_News_-_03/28/2009-138)

In September 2018, RAM retired its sole [Boeing 747-400](/wiki/Boeing_747-400 "Boeing 747-400").[[136]](#cite_note-139) In December 2018, RAM took delivery of its first [Boeing 737 MAX 8](/wiki/Boeing_737_MAX_8 "Boeing 737 MAX 8") and [Boeing 787-9](/wiki/Boeing_787-9 "Boeing 787-9").[[137]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_takes_first_Boeing_787-9-140)[[138]](#cite_note-Aircraft_Briefs-Jan._2,_2019-141)[[139]](#cite_note-142)

In August 2023, RAM signed a $300 million long-term lease contract with [Air Lease Corporation](/wiki/Air_Lease_Corporation "Air Lease Corporation") for five [Boeing 737](/wiki/Boeing_737 "Boeing 737") aircraft, including four new Boeing 737 MAX 8s and one Boeing 737-800 which are expected to be delivered in 2024.[[113]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_secures_five_B737s_from_ALC-116)

### Future plans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Future plans")]

In June 2013, RAM's CEO indicated that the airline was seeking new generation aircraft as a replacement for its ageing fleet, adding that the carrier will need some 20 to 30 new aircraft by 2020, and that the [Boeing 787](/wiki/Boeing_787 "Boeing 787") was being considered for long-haul routes, whereas the [Airbus A220](/wiki/Airbus_A220 "Airbus A220"), the [Airbus A320neo](/wiki/Airbus_A320neo "Airbus A320neo"), the [Boeing 737 MAX](/wiki/Boeing_737_MAX "Boeing 737 MAX"), and [Embraer E-Jet E2 families](/wiki/Embraer_E-Jet_E2_family "Embraer E-Jet E2 family") were all being considered for medium-haul flights.[[140]](#cite_note-Morocco's_RAM_airline_to_buy_20_new_planes_by_2020-CEO-143) A contract for the lease of four [Embraer E190s](/wiki/Embraer_E190 "Embraer E190") was signed in mid 2014;[[8]](#cite_note-airline-news-19-8) the carrier took delivery of the first of these aircraft in November the same year.[[62]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_receives_first_787_as_the_airline_finishes_restructuring_&_returns_to_profitability-63)[[141]](#cite_note-Royal_Air_Maroc_réceptionne_son_premier_Embraer_E-190-144)

As of 2023, the airline Royal Air Maroc is preparing to launch a call for tenders for new [single-aisle](/wiki/Narrow-body_aircraft "Narrow-body aircraft") and [wide-body](/wiki/Wide-body_aircraft "Wide-body aircraft") aircraft, in order to respond to the increase in tourism and strengthen the role of [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca") as a hub for sub-Saharan Africa. The airline's CEO declared on 13 June 2023 to the Bloomberg agency that RAM is “putting the final touches” to a [call for tenders](/wiki/Invitation_to_tender "Invitation to tender") for the purchase of new long and medium-haul aircraft, and plans to raise debt to help finance these acquisitions.[[142]](#cite_note-145)

## Incidents and accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Incidents and accidents")]

### Fatal accidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Fatal accidents")]

* 1 April 1970: A [Caravelle III](/wiki/Sud_Aviation_Caravelle#Variants "Sud Aviation Caravelle"), [registration](/wiki/Aircraft_registration "Aircraft registration") CN-CCV, flying the first leg of a scheduled Agadir–Casablanca–Paris flight, [crashed](/wiki/1970_Berrechid_Royal_Air_Maroc_Caravelle_crash "1970 Berrechid Royal Air Maroc Caravelle crash") on approach to [Nouasseur Airport](/wiki/Mohammed_V_International_Airport "Mohammed V International Airport") when control was lost at about 500 feet (150 m). Of the 82 people aboard, 61 perished.[[146]](#cite_note-149)[[147]](#cite_note-150)
* 22 December 1973: a leased [Caravelle VIN](/wiki/Sud_Aviation_Caravelle#Variants "Sud Aviation Caravelle"), registration OO-SRD, on the first leg of a non-scheduled Paris–Tangier–Casablanca passenger service, [crashed](/wiki/1973_Royal_Air_Maroc_Sud_Aviation_Caravelle_crash "1973 Royal Air Maroc Sud Aviation Caravelle crash") into mountainous terrain about 40 kilometres (25 mi) from [Tangier Airport](/wiki/Tangier_Ibn_Battouta_Airport "Tangier Ibn Battouta Airport") on approach. All 106 passengers and crew were killed.[[148]](#cite_note-151)
* 3 August 1975: Royal Air Maroc leased a [Boeing 707-321C](/wiki/Boeing_707 "Boeing 707") (registration JY-AEE) from [Jordanian World Airways](/wiki/Royal_Jordanian "Royal Jordanian") and was operating a [charter flight](/wiki/Charter_flight "Charter flight") from [Le Bourget Airport](/wiki/Le_Bourget_Airport "Le Bourget Airport") in Paris, France to [Inezgane Airport](/wiki/Inezgane_Airport "Inezgane Airport") in Agadir, Morocco. The aircraft approaching Agadir in poor visibility and not following a positive course, [flew into](/wiki/1975_Agadir_Royal_Air_Maroc_Boeing_707_crash "1975 Agadir Royal Air Maroc Boeing 707 crash") the Atlas Mountains, killing all 181 passengers and 7 crew members on board.[[149]](#cite_note-152)
* 21 August 1994: an [ATR 42-300](/wiki/ATR_42-300 "ATR 42-300"), registration CN-CDT, operating [Flight 630](/wiki/Royal_Air_Maroc_Flight_630 "Royal Air Maroc Flight 630") on a domestic Agadir-Casablanca route, entered a steep dive at 16,000 ft (4,900 m) and crashed into nearby mountains. Investigators concluded that the pilot deliberately disengaged the autopilot and directed the aircraft into the ground. All 44 passengers and crew died.

### Non-fatal hull losses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Air_Maroc&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Non-fatal hull losses")]

* 14 November 1958: The [undercarriage](/wiki/Landing_gear "Landing gear") of a [Douglas C-47A](/wiki/Douglas_C-47A "Douglas C-47A"), registration CN-CCJ, collapsed on landing at Tangier Airport.[[150]](#cite_note-153)
* 26 March 2003: The nosegear of a [Boeing 737-400](/wiki/Boeing_737-400 "Boeing 737-400"), registration CN-RNF, collapsed after landing at [Oujda-Les Angades Airport](/wiki/Angads_Airport "Angads Airport").[[151]](#cite_note-154)
